chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP is unreachable, so I have to work only from the textual crash entry and cannot follow the mandated detailed MCP workflow literally. I must still approximate the reasoning those steps would provide.

The crash is in __memcpy with the immediate caller NI_PublicKeyDecode, then the JNI bridge Java_com_nshc_NSaferJNI_N_1PublicKeyExport. That indicates some buffer copy during public-key decoding, likely from an input buffer into an internal structure.

Because this is a JNI-accessible function, its inputs are ultimately controlled by Java. There is no Java call graph provided, so I cannot see how the Java side validates the arguments. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input replaces the usual Java entrypoint, so the fuzzer may be feeding arbitrary data directly into the JNI method.

Nonetheless, the pattern __memcpy -> NI_PublicKeyDecode from fuzz inputs strongly suggests a classic length/size mismatch or missing bounds check during decoding/parsing of attacker-controlled key blobs. Public key decoding is inherently parsing of external data (certificates, keys, etc.), which in a real app often originates from untrusted sources (smart cards, network, files).

Allocator/sanitizer frames are not shown; the top native frame is __memcpy itself, with NI_PublicKeyDecode as the first app-level frame. A memcpy crash is almost always memory safety (out-of-bounds read or write) unless explicitly guarded by checked lengths, which I cannot verify here. The presence of fuzzing-induced crash reinforces this.

Given that the crash is not in the harness but in a core library function that would be used in production, and that its inputs are plausibly attacker-controlled (decoded public keys), I should classify this as a likely memory corruption vulnerability.

Without code, I cannot state severity as critical (RCE) with high confidence, but an unchecked memcpy in a key parser is at least high severity because it can lead to out-of-bounds write/read and DoS, and possibly code execution, depending on context.

I’ll mark it vulnerable with medium-to-high confidence (~0.75), cite CWE-787/119, and note limitations: no decompilation, no Java callgraph, no proof of exact buffer sizes. I’ll keep call_sequence based on the stack trace.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy called from NI_PublicKeyDecode indicates out-of-bounds memory access during key decoding
Function is reachable via JNI bridge Java_com_nshc_NSaferJNI_N_1PublicKeyExport and receives fuzzed input, implying attacker-controlled data reaches memcpy length/pointers
Public key decoding of untrusted blobs is realistic in production (smart card operations), so the same path is likely reachable outside the fuzz harness

evidence.note: Top application frame before __memcpy in native stack trace, likely performing unsafe copy while decoding a public key blob.
JNI entrypoint that exposes NI_PublicKeyDecode to Java and, in fuzzing, to arbitrary attacker-controlled input.

recommendations: Review and decompile NI_PublicKeyDecode to locate memcpy/memmove calls and ensure all lengths are derived from validated buffer sizes
Add explicit bounds checks before copying public key data, using the actual allocated destination buffer size and parsed input length
Augment fuzzing with ASan/UBSan builds of libNSaferJNI.so to precisely identify the overflowing memcpy and validate fixes
assumptions: Public key data passed into NI_PublicKeyDecode originates from untrusted or partially untrusted sources in real app flows
No comprehensive bounds checking is performed before the crashing memcpy, given the observed crash under fuzzing
The fuzz harness calls the JNI method in a way that is representative of real JNI usage (argument types/order)
limitations: Could not use MCP decompilation tools due to connection errors; no direct view of NI_PublicKeyDecode implementation
Java call graph for the JNI method is empty, so actual argument construction and validation on the Java side is unknown
Crash site details (exact memcpy arguments, whether read or write caused the fault) are not available, so exploitability details are inferred
